---
title: "3.2 Bollinger Bands"
subtitle: "Chapter 03. Implementing Mean Reversion Strategies"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: false
include-after-body:
  - ../../../../styles/web-note-modal.html
---

::: {.section-output-links}
[Slides](video-lesson-slides.qmd){.action-video aria-label="Open video lesson slides" data-modal="video" data-label="Video lesson slides" data-tooltip="Slides"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[PDF](pdf-notes.qmd){.action-pdf aria-label="Open PDF notes" data-tooltip="PDF"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Markdown](downloads/section.json){.action-markdown aria-label="Open raw markdown viewer" data-modal="markdown" data-label="Markdown" data-tooltip="Markdown" data-markdown-src="downloads/section.json"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Bundle](downloads/section-bundle.zip){.action-bundle aria-label="Download markdown bundle" data-tooltip="Bundle"}
:::

# Bollinger Bands Turn a Demonstration Strategy into a Tradable Rule

The linear strategy from Chapter 2 is intentionally simple: position size is
proportional to the negative Z-score of the spread or portfolio price. That
rule is useful because it is nearly parameterless and makes the mean-reversion
logic transparent. But Chan emphasizes a practical defect: the strategy has no
natural bound on capital usage. If the spread keeps moving away from the mean,
the desired position keeps growing.

Bollinger bands keep the same mean-and-volatility framework but convert it into
a threshold rule with bounded exposure.

# The Z-Score Stays the Same

Let the portfolio price be $y_{\text{port}}$. Using a lookback window $L$, the
standardized deviation is

$$
z_t
= \frac{y_{\text{port},t} - \operatorname{movingAvg}(y_{\text{port},t};L)}
         {\operatorname{movingStd}(y_{\text{port},t};L)}.
$$

This is the same Z-score used in the linear strategy. What changes is how it is
mapped into trades.

# Entry and Exit Thresholds Replace Continuous Scaling

Choose an entry threshold $\text{entryZscore}$ and an exit threshold
$\text{exitZscore}$ with

$$
\text{exitZscore} < \text{entryZscore}.
$$

Then the trading logic is:

- enter long when $z_t < -\text{entryZscore}$
- exit long when $z_t \ge -\text{exitZscore}$
- enter short when $z_t > \text{entryZscore}$
- exit short when $z_t \le \text{exitZscore}$

If $\text{exitZscore}=0$, the strategy exits when the spread returns to the
current estimated mean. If $\text{exitZscore}=-\text{entryZscore}$, the exit
requires the spread to continue all the way through to the opposite band.

At any moment the position is typically one of three states:

- no position
- long one unit portfolio
- short one unit portfolio

This is what makes the strategy easy to budget and risk-manage.

# Lookback Still Comes from the Reversion Time Scale

The threshold logic introduces new parameters, but the time-scale logic from
Chapter 2 still applies. Chan allows the lookback to be either optimized on
training data or anchored to the half-life of mean reversion.

That is an important design choice. The half-life determines how quickly the
spread forgets deviations. The lookback should therefore still reflect the
reversion horizon, even if entries and exits are now discrete rather than
continuous.

# Why Bounded Exposure Can Improve Performance

A common intuition is that the linear strategy should always be better because
it scales harder into larger deviations. Chan's GLD-USO example shows why that
intuition can fail. The bounded Bollinger rule produced better performance than
the unconstrained linear rule in that case.

The reason is not mysterious. The linear rule keeps adding exposure when the
deviation widens, whether or not the widening is likely to revert soon. The
Bollinger rule waits for a large-enough deviation to justify entry, then limits
the trade to one unit. That can improve realized performance when large
deviations are not reliably rewarded by still larger position sizes.

# The Practical Appeal Is as Important as the Backtest

Chan stresses a second benefit that is independent of the example's Sharpe
ratio. The Bollinger strategy is easy to allocate capital to. A portfolio
manager can reserve capital for one unit long or one unit short. This is much
harder to do for a strategy whose exposure is an unbounded function of Z-score.

That operational simplicity is part of the strategy design, not a minor
implementation afterthought.

# Review Checklist

- Is the Z-score built from the same spread definition chosen in Section 3.1?
- Is the lookback economically tied to the reversion horizon?
- Are entry and exit thresholds clearly separated?
- Is the portfolio sized in bounded units rather than open-ended scaling?
- Does the practical capital budget match the one-unit assumption in the rule?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [3.1 Trading Pairs Using Price Spreads, Log Price Spreads, or Ratios](../01-trading-pairs-using-price-spreads-log-price-spreads-or-ratios/web-notes.qmd)
- Next: [3.3 Does Scaling-in Work?](../03-does-scaling-in-work/web-notes.qmd)
